QF.id: Clubbing, Set-Off and Carry Forward of Losses_2

question.text: Rekha has a 25% share in the profits of M/s Dhanush Enterprises, a partnership firm. Her husband Mohan is employed by the same firm and receives a salary of Rs.6,00,000 per year. Mohan does not hold any professional qualifications relevant to his role. In whose hands will Mohan's salary be clubbed?

answer.text: Mohan's income, since salary is always taxable in the hands of the person earning it
Shared equally between Rekha and Mohan
The partnership firm's income, as it is a disallowed expense
Rekha's income, as she has substantial interest in the concern employing Mohan and he lacks relevant professional qualifications

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Section 64(1)(ii), if a spouse has substantial interest (20%+ voting power or profit share) in a concern where the other spouse is employed, the remuneration of the employed spouse is clubbed with the spouse having substantial interest — UNLESS the employed spouse has professional qualifications and the income is solely due to those qualifications. Rekha holds 25% (>20%), so she has substantial interest, and Mohan lacks relevant qualifications. **The Trap:** The exception for professional qualifications is critical. If Mohan were a qualified CA or engineer and the role required that qualification, the salary would remain in his hands. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A covers the concept of substantial interest in a simpler context. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Before structuring family employment in a business, verify if the employed family member has bona fide professional qualifications — it can make a Rs.1,80,000 slab difference in tax.
